rationale_for_baseline_tier: An intensely itchy but systemically well patient with a benign non-contagious autoimmune rash needs prompt in-person confirmation and symptom relief — a T2 baseline. Low acuity; the only escalation is for secondary skin infection.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Scratched areas becoming hot, spreading red, draining pus, or fever
escalation_edges.rationale: Indicates secondary bacterial infection needing antibiotics.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If the scratched skin becomes hot, spreads red, or oozes pus, or you get a fever, see a doctor the same day.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Lives alone with severe sleep loss
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Prioritise effective nocturnal itch control to restore sleep and function.

baseline_safety_net_advice.default_tier_advice: This is a non-contagious autoimmune rash (lichen planus) — no need to treat your house. Use the steroid cream and a night antihistamine to settle the itch and sleep, keep nails short, and see your GP within a week to confirm. See a doctor sooner if the scratched skin becomes hot, spreading red, or oozes pus.
baseline_safety_net_advice.contact_method: GP within 7-10 days; same-day if infection signs
baseline_safety_net_advice.after_hours_advice: No after-hours care needed unless secondary infection develops.
baseline_safety_net_advice.documentation_for_gp: Telehealth impression of cutaneous lichen planus (violaceous flat-topped papules, Wickham striae, no burrows/contacts); reassured non-contagious; potent topical steroid + night antihistamine advised; in-person confirmation/biopsy recommended.

critical_under_triage.failure_description: Dismissing without confirmation or a secondary-infection safety-net.
critical_under_triage.patient_harm_scenario: Secondary infection of excoriated skin is missed and progresses to cellulitis.

moderate_over_triage.failure_description: Household scabies treatment or ED escalation for a benign itchy rash is over-triage.
